1.name: Chief Growth Officer
1.mission: I set the optimization rules, define what 'good' looks like, and make sure every decision the system makes aligns with how we actually want to grow this business. I'm the one who decides when the system is ready to do more on its own. I operate as the executive reasoning layer: synthesizing 13 domain scans into unified daily intelligence, producing and prioritizing ChangeSets through a three-layer architecture, synthesizing competitive fragments daily and conducting strategic competitive analysis monthly/quarterly, and routing decisions to Devon/Nick/Katie/Peter within defined authority boundaries.

2.name: Data Intelligence Lead
2.mission: I'm the person who knows the numbers. I connect every data source, build the dashboards, generate the reports, and flag the anomalies. When someone asks 'how are we doing?' — I answer.

3.name: Operations Intelligence Lead
3.mission: I monitor every operation—from pipelines to customer success. I see the patterns in what we're doing, measure efficiency, flag blockages, and ensure our operational activities stay aligned with growth goals.

4.name: Growth Activation Lead
4.mission: I take insights from the data, guidance from the system rules, and turn them into actions. If we identify a growth lever, I test it. If we find a bottleneck, I implement the solution. I'm the bridge between knowing and doing.

5.name: Product Engineering Lead
5.mission: I build and iterate on the product itself. When the system identifies a feature gap, I engineer it. When we need to automate manual work, I code it. I turn strategic needs into shipped features.

6.name: Infrastructure & Operations Lead
6.mission: I keep the systems running. I manage infrastructure, security, compliance, and reliability. I ensure that whatever the team builds stays stable and scales as we grow.

7.name: Customer Success Lead
7.mission: I'm the voice of the customer inside the system. I gather feedback, track satisfaction, identify churn risks, and ensure we're solving real problems. Every decision should make our customers happier.

8.name: Demand Generation Lead
8.mission: I drive awareness and demand for our product. I own marketing, content, partnerships, and outreach. I make sure the right people know about us and want to try us.

9.name: Revenue Operations Lead
9.mission: I optimize how we acquire and retain customers. I own pricing, GTM motion, sales enablement, and retention mechanics. I make sure revenue flows align with growth strategy.

10.name: Finance & Planning Lead
10.mission: I translate business strategy into financial reality. I build forecasts, allocate budget, track burn rate, and ensure we're using capital efficiently to reach our next milestone.

11.name: Brand & Culture Lead
11.mission: I define who we are as a company. I build brand identity, shape company culture, and ensure our external story matches our internal reality. People want to work here because they believe in us.

A.name: Pulse Review Pattern
A.description: Every role starts the day the same way: receive refreshed data, review role-specific metrics against thresholds, flag alerts (Red/Yellow/Green), log actions or escalate cross-role. This pattern standardizes the daily heartbeat across the entire system.

steps.action: Receive refreshed data mart(s) after daily dbt run completes (typically 6:00-6:30 AM UTC). Mart data is current as of prior business day close.

Review role-specific metrics panel against configured thresholds: **CGO** (revenue, CVR, CAC, LTV, inventory, cash position), **Paid Media** (spend, CPM, CPC, ROAS by platform), **Amazon** (FBA/FBM revenue, return rate, Best Seller status), **PM** (ChangeSet queue depth, experiment run count, system health alerts).
Flag items exceeding threshold as alerts: **RED** (immediate escalation required, e.g., revenue drop >15%), **YELLOW** (monitor trend, investigate if persists 2+ days).

Log flags as tasks (for own-role action) or escalations (for cross-role impact). Document context: what metric triggered, what threshold was exceeded, what happened yesterday that could explain it (e.g., campaign launch, ChangeSet implementation, external event).

If cross-role impact detected (e.g., Paid Media lead notices unusual inventory spike), route alert to relevant role owner (e.g., Channel Execution Manager) with context. If metric clearly linked to recent ChangeSet, flag for review cycle decision.

A.inputs: Data Intelligence Lead (Daily Pulse report, Monday Growth Review); Operations Intelligence Lead (Optimization Status dashboard, anomaly flags)
A.outputs: Role-specific alerts; Daily Pulse report (email + Slack); Operations Intelligence Lead (if escalation needed); Edge Case Log (if conflict/decision required)
A.humanGate: Role Leader review and categorization of alerts
A.decisionPoints: Is this a known event (campaign launch, test implementation, seasonal pattern)? Is cross-role impact detected? Does metric require immediate action (RED threshold) or monitoring (YELLOW threshold)?
A.specReferences: Data Brain §6.1 (Daily Pulse), §6.2 (anomaly detection); Ops Intelligence §5.1 (guardrail thresholds)
A.shortcut: Varies by role — see individual process shortcuts below

B.name: Brief Request Pattern
B.description: When any role identifies a creative need — from a content gap, performance signal, or campaign requirement — it flows through this pattern. The requesting role defines the signal and priority, then /creative-brief routes it to the Creative Strategist (4a.3), who owns brief generation, production routing, and quality control.

steps.action: Identify creative need: What is the source signal (gap identification, opportunity, risk mitigation)? What is the business context (campaign, product launch, inventory trigger, experimentation need)?

Determine priority level based on urgency: **Urgent** (inventory-driven, product launch, <48h timeline), **Standard** (regular campaign, 7-14 day timeline), **Backlog** (exploratory, no firm deadline).

Populate brief request schema: source_signal (clear statement of need + context), priority level, objective (desired behavior change), surface (where displayed), segment (target audience), product_type, offer (discount/free/none), claim_tier (Tier 0-3 if applicable). Do NOT generate full Creative Brief Object schema (hooks, concepts, variants, SLA, cross-channel specs) — that is Creative Strategist's responsibility.
Submit request to Creative Strategist (4a.3) via /creative-brief shortcut or queue. Requesting role becomes the source_signal owner (available for follow-up questions during brief generation).

Creative Strategist (4a.3) receives request, validates demand signal, populates complete Creative Brief Object with all schema fields (hooks, concepts, variants, SLA, cross-channel specs, proof_links).

Creative Strategist owns full brief generation, production routing to Content Producer (4b), quality gates (claims review if Tier 2+), and SLA tracking. Requesting role tracks brief status and SLA compliance.
When creative asset delivered, requesting role deploys through its own workflow (PR review → deploy for web, ChangeSet implementation for channel, etc.).

B.inputs: Requesting Role (source_signal, priority context); Creative Strategist (demand signal validation)
B.outputs: Creative Brief Object (complete schema); Creative assets (routed to Content Producer); Brief status tracking (available to requesting role)
B.humanGate: Requesting Role defines need; Creative Strategist validates demand signal and gates brief generation
B.decisionPoints: Is demand signal clear and actionable? Is priority level correct given timeline and impact? Does Creative Strategist have capacity for SLA?
B.specReferences: Creative Intelligence §4 (demand signal consumption); Website Opt Engine §9.3 (Creative Brief Object schema); Channel Execution §8 (brief lifecycle)
B.shortcut: /creative-brief routes to Creative Strategist 4a.3 (steps 3-4 automated; step 1-2 manual by requesting role)

processes.name: Creative Brief Generation for Web Content (Streamlined)

C.name: Learning Record Pattern
C.description: When a process produces a meaningful insight — from an A/B test, a ChangeSet measurement, a campaign result — this pattern captures it. The process owner documents what changed, what happened, and the evidence, then /learning-record routes it to the System Learning Protocol (3.8), which evaluates confidence and routes to Performance Memory or skill file updates. This is how ChangeSet results and experiment outcomes become institutional knowledge.

steps.action: Identify learning threshold: Has this learning met the bar for documentation? (5+ data points showing consistent pattern, >30% median outperformance vs. baseline, >80% confidence level in pattern, actionable insight for future execution).

If threshold met, produce standardized learning record with schema: what changed (variable tested, hypothesis, execution change), outcome data (lift %, sample size, statistical significance if applicable), context (date, duration, segment), what we'd do differently next time (updated rule, template change, priority shift).
Include supporting evidence: link to performance data, A/B test results if applicable, competitor/historical benchmarks that contextualize the finding.

Submit learning record to System Learning Protocol (3.8) via /learning-record shortcut or queue. Do NOT independently push to skill files, learning libraries, or trigger updates to Content Producer (Larry 2.0) templates.
System Learning Protocol (3.8) receives record, evaluates for confidence level and impact scope.

Protocol 3.8 routes learning record downstream: to Performance Memory (for historical pattern matching), to skill file update queue (if high-confidence actionable for Larry 2.0 template evolution), to learning notes library (for searchable reference), or to learning validation queue (if confidence borderline).
Protocol 3.8 gates all routing decisions, ensuring consistent standards and avoiding duplication across multiple learning pipelines.

C.inputs: Process Owner (learning record schema); System Learning Protocol (confidence evaluation)
C.outputs: Learning record (routed to appropriate destination); Performance Memory updates (if applicable); Skill file update queue (if actionable)
C.humanGate: Process Owner identifies and documents learning; System Learning Protocol (3.8) gates all routing decisions
C.decisionPoints: Has learning threshold been met (5+ points, >30% outperformance)? Is confidence level high enough for routing (>80%)? What is the scope of impact (single product, all products, single channel, cross-channel)?
C.specReferences: Ops Intelligence §9 (System Learning Protocol), §9.8 (learning record schema); Creative Intelligence §11 (Performance Measurement for context)
C.shortcut: /learning-record submits to System Learning Protocol 3.8 (step 4 automated; steps 1-3 manual by process owner)
